title: Confessions of a Reluctant Protein-Maxxer
gist: A first-person essay on America's 'protein-maxxing' craze describes how a viral social-media trend prioritizing protein at every meal has moved from gym/fitness culture into mainstream grocery aisles, with high-protein labels now on snack bars, pancake mix, ice cream, chips, and pastries.

insights.claim: 'Protein-maxxing' — a viral social media trend of prioritizing protein at every meal and snack — has spread far beyond gym and fitness-optimization culture into the mainstream grocery market, with high-protein labels now appearing on snack bars, pancake mix, ice cream, snack chips, and pastries.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: A consumer packaged-goods trend worth tracking: high-protein reformulation is being applied across categories (snacks, desserts, breakfast) well beyond traditional sports-nutrition products, signaling broad CPG repositioning opportunity.
insights.locator: But I quickly realized it had spread far beyond

insights.claim: Social media is described as awash with influencers aggressively marketing protein supplements to a mainstream audience, extending well beyond the traditional 'gym bro' demographic.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Influencer-driven demand generation for protein supplements suggests continued growth runway for the category via social-commerce channels rather than traditional retail marketing.
insights.locator: Social media is awash with influencers aggressively hawking protein supplements

insights.claim: The author frames the protein-maxxing trend around the concept of 'body recomposition' — replacing fat with muscle via resistance training fueled by adequate protein intake — as the underlying goal driving personal engagement with the trend after a period of enforced inactivity (a broken foot) led to muscle loss and weight gain.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Illustrates the underlying health narrative (recomposition, not just weight loss) that food marketers are tapping into to sell high-protein products to a broader-than-fitness audience.
